ocr.text: This report examines the impact of varying poring pulse voltages on embryo development and viability, as evidenced by the
data presented in Table 1. The study focused on the WKY strain and assessed multiple parameters, including the number of >2-
cell embryos (A) and the number of embryos exhibiting fluorescence (B).
Stain  Poring Pulse Voltage (V)  Transfer pulseNo. of Pulses  No. of > 2-cell embryos (A)  No. of embryos with fluorescence (B)  % (B/A)
A total of 50 volts were applied during the poring pulse phase, which was followed by three transfer pulses. The data indicates
that a significant number of embryos, specifically 138, reached the >2-cell stage, highlighting a positive outcome in terms of
developmental success. Furthermore, the number of embryos displaying fluorescence, which is crucial for evaluating successful
genetic modifications, stood at 68. This resulted in a fluorescence percentage of approximately 49.3%, calculated as (B/A) *
100. The correlation between the applied voltage and the observed outcomes underscores the necessity of optimizing electrical
parameters to enhance embryo viability and genetic expression. Consequently, these findings contribute to a deeper
understanding of the electrophysiological processes involved in embryo development and provide a framework for future
research aimed at improving genetic engineering techniques in reproductive biotechnology.
